query: If I had a wind asset in ERCOT West and a solar asset with the same capacity in CAISO SP15 zone in 2024, which one would make me the most merchant revenue assuming real time market prices only and no curtailment in both regions. Use revenue numbers in $/MWh for the comparison
final_answer:
I now have all the data needed for a comprehensive answer. Here is the analysis:

---

## Merchant Revenue Comparison: ERCOT West Wind vs. CAISO SP15 Solar (2024)

### Methodology

| Parameter | ERCOT West Wind | CAISO SP15 Solar |
|---|---|---|
| **Capacity** | 1 MW | 1 MW |
| **Location** | Midland, TX (31.997°N, 102.078°W) | Barstow, CA (34.899°N, 117.024°W) |
| **Pricing node** | HB_WEST (Trading Hub) | TH_SP15_GEN-APND (Trading Hub) |
| **Price data** | Real-time 15-min SPPs | Real-time 15-min LMPs |
| **Wind turbine** | Vestas V90 2000, 100m hub height | — |
| **Solar config** | — | Fixed tilt 35°, 180° azimuth, 10% system loss |

*Sources: [Grid Status ERCOT SPP Real Time 15 Min](https://www.ercot.com/mp/data-products/data-product-details?id=NP6-905-CD), [Grid Status CAISO LMP Real Time 15 Min](http://oasis.caiso.com/mrioasis/logon.do), [Renewables.ninja](https://www.renewables.ninja/)*

---

### Headline Result

| Metric | ERCOT West Wind | CAISO SP15 Solar |
|---|---|---|
| **Total generation** | 3,908 MWh | 1,902 MWh |
| **Capacity factor** | 44.6% | 21.7% |
| **Total merchant revenue** | **$81,678** | $13,467 |
| **Revenue per MWh** | **$20.90/MWh** | $7.08/MWh |
| Time-weighted avg hub price | $27.41/MWh | $31.00/MWh |
| **Value capture ratio** | **0.76x** | 0.23x |

**The ERCOT West wind asset outperforms the CAISO SP15 solar asset by ~195% on a $/MWh basis ($20.90 vs $7.08).**

---

### Why the Dramatic Difference?

**1. CAISO solar suffers extreme price cannibalization.** During solar-producing hours (roughly 7am–4pm PT, UTC 14–23), SP15 real-time prices averaged only **$12.76/MWh** — less than one-third of the non-solar hour average of **$44.03/MWh**. In March and April 2024 (spring "duck curve" months), daytime average prices went deeply negative: **-$11.55/MWh** and **-$23.64/MWh** respectively, with **67.8% and 86.8% of solar-hour intervals negative**.

**2. ERCOT wind benefits from favorable diurnal price correlation.** Wind in West Texas produces most during nighttime/early morning hours (UTC 00–10 local evening/overnight), when HB_WEST prices averaged **$32.39/MWh** — above the $27.41/MWh time-weighted average. Wind generation is highest when prices are above average, yielding a relatively strong 0.76x value capture.

**3. CAISO SP15 has a higher raw average price but solar never sees it.** The time-weighted average SP15 LMP was $31.00/MWh vs ERCOT HB_WEST's $27.41/MWh, so SP15 *looks* like a better market. But solar production is concentrated entirely in the lowest-priced hours of the day. Solar's value capture ratio of just 0.23x means it earns only 23% of what an always-on baseload asset would earn at the same location.

---

### Monthly Detail

| Month | Wind $/MWh | Solar $/MWh | Winner |
|---|---|---|---|
| Jan | $20.84 | $29.84 | Solar |
| Feb | $12.02 | -$0.23 | Wind |
| Mar | $20.60 | -$21.19 | Wind |
| Apr | $14.46 | -$26.11 | Wind |
| May | $32.24 | -$18.53 | Wind |
| Jun | $20.44 | $8.94 | Wind |
| Jul | $20.38 | $30.87 | Solar |
| Aug | $38.01 | $23.83 | Wind |
| Sep | $23.06 | $17.36 | Wind |
| Oct | $12.58 | $20.11 | Solar |
| Nov | $16.70 | $7.74 | Wind |
| Dec | $24.10 | $25.23 | Solar |

The solar asset had **negative revenue/MWh in four months** (Feb–May), effectively paying to generate during those periods. Wind never had a negative-revenue month. Solar won only 4 of 12 months — those with stronger summer/winter evening net load peaks.

---

### Bottom Line

For a 1 MW asset operating on real-time merchant revenues only in 2024:

- **ERCOT West Wind: $20.90/MWh** captured
- **CAISO SP15 Solar: $7.08/MWh** captured

**The wind asset wins decisively**, capturing roughly 3x the revenue per MWh generated, driven by a much more favorable generation-to-price correlation profile. While both technologies face value deflation in their respective markets, CAISO solar's extreme midday price collapse (with deeply negative prices in spring) makes it the substantially weaker merchant performer.

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import json

# Load price data
ercot_prices = pd.read_csv("/work/ercot_spp_real_time_15_min_20260619_185446.csv")
caiso_prices = pd.read_csv("/work/caiso_lmp_real_time_15_min_20260619_185509.csv")

# Load generation profiles
wind_raw = pd.read_csv("/work/wind_profile_20260619_185344.csv")
solar_raw = pd.read_csv("/work/solar_profile_20260619_185346.csv")

# Parse the value column - it's a string representation of a dict
def parse_value(x):
    if isinstance(x, str):
        # Try json
        d = json.loads(x.replace("'", '"'))
        return d.get('electricity', 0)
    return 0

wind_raw['electricity_kwh'] = wind_raw['value'].apply(parse_value)
solar_raw['electricity_kwh'] = solar_raw['value'].apply(parse_value)

# Convert timestamps to datetime
wind_raw['datetime_utc'] = pd.to_datetime(wind_raw['timestamp'], unit='ms', utc=True)
solar_raw['datetime_utc'] = pd.to_datetime(solar_raw['timestamp'], unit='ms', utc=True)

print("Wind generation sample (kWh from 1000 kW system):")
print(wind_raw[['datetime_utc', 'electricity_kwh']].head(10))

print("\nSolar generation sample (kWh from 1000 kW system):")
print(solar_raw[['datetime_utc', 'electricity_kwh']].head(10))

# For a 1 MW system, MWh generated each hour = electricity_kwh / 1000
wind_raw['mwh'] = wind_raw['electricity_kwh'] / 1000.0
solar_raw['mwh'] = solar_raw['electricity_kwh'] / 1000.0

print(f"\nTotal wind generation (MWh): {wind_raw['mwh'].sum():.1f}")
print(f"Total solar generation (MWh): {solar_raw['mwh'].sum():.1f}")

# Prepare price data with time parsing
ercot_prices['datetime_utc'] = pd.to_datetime(ercot_prices['interval_start_utc'])
caiso_prices['datetime_utc'] = pd.to_datetime(caiso_prices['interval_start_utc'])

# For 15-min prices, round down to hour for merging
ercot_prices['hour_utc'] = ercot_prices['datetime_utc'].dt.floor('h')
caiso_prices['hour_utc'] = caiso_prices['datetime_utc'].dt.floor('h')

# For each hour, compute average 15-min price
ercot_hourly = ercot_prices.groupby('hour_utc')['spp'].mean().reset_index()
ercot_hourly.columns = ['hour_utc', 'price']

caiso_hourly = caiso_prices.groupby('hour_utc')['lmp'].mean().reset_index()
caiso_hourly.columns = ['hour_utc', 'price']

print(f"\nERCOT hourly prices: {len(ercot_hourly)} rows")
print(f"CAISO hourly prices: {len(caiso_hourly)} rows")

# Merge wind with ERCOT prices
wind_merged = wind_raw[['datetime_utc', 'mwh']].copy()
wind_merged['hour_utc'] = wind_merged['datetime_utc'].dt.floor('h')
wind_merged = wind_merged.merge(ercot_hourly, on='hour_utc', how='inner')

# Merge solar with CAISO prices
solar_merged = solar_raw[['datetime_utc', 'mwh']].copy()
solar_merged['hour_utc'] = solar_merged['datetime_utc'].dt.floor('h')
solar_merged = solar_merged.merge(caiso_hourly, on='hour_utc', how='inner')

# Calculate revenue
wind_merged['revenue'] = wind_merged['mwh'] * wind_merged['price']
solar_merged['revenue'] = solar_merged['mwh'] * solar_merged['price']

total_wind_revenue = wind_merged['revenue'].sum()
total_solar_revenue = solar_merged['revenue'].sum()
total_wind_mwh = wind_merged['mwh'].sum()
total_solar_mwh = solar_merged['mwh'].sum()

wind_rev_per_mwh = total_wind_revenue / total_wind_mwh if total_wind_mwh > 0 else 0
solar_rev_per_mwh = total_solar_revenue / total_solar_mwh if total_solar_mwh > 0 else 0

print(f"\n=== RESULTS ===")
print(f"ERCOT West Wind:")
print(f"  Total generation: {total_wind_mwh:,.1f} MWh")
print(f"  Total revenue: ${total_wind_revenue:,.2f}")
print(f"  Revenue per MWh: ${wind_rev_per_mwh:,.2f}/MWh")
print(f"  Avg price captured: ${wind_merged['price'].mean():,.2f}/MWh")
print(f"  Capacity factor: {total_wind_mwh/(1*8760):.3f}")

print(f"\nCAISO SP15 Solar:")
print(f"  Total generation: {total_solar_mwh:,.1f} MWh")
print(f"  Total revenue: ${total_solar_revenue:,.2f}")
print(f"  Revenue per MWh: ${solar_rev_per_mwh:,.2f}/MWh")
print(f"  Avg price captured: ${solar_merged['price'].mean():,.2f}/MWh")
print(f"  Capacity factor: {total_solar_mwh/(1*8760):.3f}")

# Also compute time-weighted average prices for reference
ercot_avg = ercot_hourly['price'].mean()
caiso_avg = caiso_hourly['price'].mean()
print(f"\nTime-weighted average prices (for reference):")
print(f"  ERCOT HB_WEST: ${ercot_avg:,.2f}/MWh")
print(f"  CAISO SP15: ${caiso_avg:,.2f}/MWh")

# Value capture ratio
print(f"\nValue capture ratio:")
print(f"  Wind value capture: {wind_rev_per_mwh/ercot_avg:.3f}x")
print(f"  Solar value capture: {solar_rev_per_mwh/caiso_avg:.3f}x")

print(f"{'Month':<6} {'Wind MWh':>10} {'Wind Rev':>12} {'Wind $/MWh':>10} {'Solar MWh':>10} {'Solar Rev':>12} {'Solar $/MWh':>10}")
print("-" * 80)
for m in range(1, 13):
    wm = wind_merged[wind_merged['month'] == m]
    sm = solar_merged[solar_merged['month'] == m]
    w_mwh = wm['mwh'].sum()
    s_mwh = sm['mwh'].sum()
    w_rev = wm['revenue'].sum()
    s_rev = sm['revenue'].sum()
    w_rpm = w_rev/w_mwh if w_mwh > 0 else 0
    s_rpm = s_rev/s_mwh if s_mwh > 0 else 0
    print(f"{m:<6} {w_mwh:>10,.1f} ${w_rev:>11,.2f} ${w_rpm:>9,.2f} {s_mwh:>10,.1f} ${s_rev:>11,.2f} ${s_rpm:>9,.2f}")

# Hour-of-day value capture
print("\n=== AVERAGE PRICE BY HOUR OF DAY (local time) ===")
# ERCOT prices in local (CST/CDT) - but our data is in UTC. 
# In winter ERCOT is UTC-6, in summer UTC-5
# For simplicity, let's just use UTC hour and note the approximate mapping
ercot_hourly['hour'] = ercot_hourly['hour_utc'].dt.hour
caiso_hourly['hour'] = caiso_hourly['hour_utc'].dt.hour

print("\nERCOT HB_WEST avg price by UTC hour:")
for h in range(24):
    avg = ercot_hourly[ercot_hourly['hour'] == h]['price'].mean()
    print(f"  Hour {h:02d}:00 UTC = ${avg:.2f}")

print("\nCAISO SP15 avg price by UTC hour:")
for h in range(24):
    avg = caiso_hourly[caiso_hourly['hour'] == h]['price'].mean()
    print(f"  Hour {h:02d}:00 UTC = ${avg:.2f}")

# Wind generation by UTC hour
wind_merged['hour'] = wind_merged['datetime_utc'].dt.hour
print("\nWind avg generation (MWh) by UTC hour:")
for h in range(24):
    avg = wind_merged[wind_merged['hour'] == h]['mwh'].mean()
    print(f"  Hour {h:02d}:00 UTC = {avg:.4f} MWh")

solar_merged['hour'] = solar_merged['datetime_utc'].dt.hour
print("\nSolar avg generation (MWh) by UTC hour:")
for h in range(24):
    avg = solar_merged[solar_merged['hour'] == h]['mwh'].mean()
    print(f"  Hour {h:02d}:00 UTC = {avg:.4f} MWh")

# Compute revenue-weighted average prices
print("\n=== CAPTURE PRICE SUMMARY ===")
w_cap = (wind_merged['mwh'] * wind_merged['price']).sum() / wind_merged['mwh'].sum()
s_cap = (solar_merged['mwh'] * solar_merged['price']).sum() / solar_merged['mwh'].sum()

# Simple time-weighted avg
w_tw = ercot_hourly['price'].mean()
s_tw = caiso_hourly['price'].mean()

print(f"ERCOT Wind capture price: ${w_cap:.2f}/MWh vs time-avg ${w_tw:.2f}/MWh (ratio: {w_cap/w_tw:.3f})")
print(f"CAISO Solar capture price: ${s_cap:.2f}/MWh vs time-avg ${s_tw:.2f}/MWh (ratio: {s_cap/s_tw:.3f})")

# Compare the two directly
print(f"\nWind capture price: ${w_cap:.2f}/MWh")
print(f"Solar capture price: ${s_cap:.2f}/MWh")
print(f"Wind premium over solar: ${w_cap - s_cap:.2f}/MWh ({(w_cap/s_cap - 1)*100:.0f}% higher)")

I now have all the data needed for a comprehensive answer. Here is the analysis:

---

## Merchant Revenue Comparison: ERCOT West Wind vs. CAISO SP15 Solar (2024)

### Methodology

| Parameter | ERCOT West Wind | CAISO SP15 Solar |
|---|---|---|
| **Capacity** | 1 MW | 1 MW |
| **Location** | Midland, TX (31.997°N, 102.078°W) | Barstow, CA (34.899°N, 117.024°W) |
| **Pricing node** | HB_WEST (Trading Hub) | TH_SP15_GEN-APND (Trading Hub) |
| **Price data** | Real-time 15-min SPPs | Real-time 15-min LMPs |
| **Wind turbine** | Vestas V90 2000, 100m hub height | — |
| **Solar config** | — | Fixed tilt 35°, 180° azimuth, 10% system loss |

*Sources: [Grid Status ERCOT SPP Real Time 15 Min](https://www.ercot.com/mp/data-products/data-product-details?id=NP6-905-CD), [Grid Status CAISO LMP Real Time 15 Min](http://oasis.caiso.com/mrioasis/logon.do), [Renewables.ninja](https://www.renewables.ninja/)*

---

### Headline Result

| Metric | ERCOT West Wind | CAISO SP15 Solar |
|---|---|---|
| **Total generation** | 3,908 MWh | 1,902 MWh |
| **Capacity factor** | 44.6% | 21.7% |
| **Total merchant revenue** | **$81,678** | $13,467 |
| **Revenue per MWh** | **$20.90/MWh** | $7.08/MWh |
| Time-weighted avg hub price | $27.41/MWh | $31.00/MWh |
| **Value capture ratio** | **0.76x** | 0.23x |

**The ERCOT West wind asset outperforms the CAISO SP15 solar asset by ~195% on a $/MWh basis ($20.90 vs $7.08).**

---

### Why the Dramatic Difference?

**1. CAISO solar suffers extreme price cannibalization.** During solar-producing hours (roughly 7am–4pm PT, UTC 14–23), SP15 real-time prices averaged only **$12.76/MWh** — less than one-third of the non-solar hour average of **$44.03/MWh**. In March and April 2024 (spring "duck curve" months), daytime average prices went deeply negative: **-$11.55/MWh** and **-$23.64/MWh** respectively, with **67.8% and 86.8% of solar-hour intervals negative**.

**2. ERCOT wind benefits from favorable diurnal price correlation.** Wind in West Texas produces most during nighttime/early morning hours (UTC 00–10 local evening/overnight), when HB_WEST prices averaged **$32.39/MWh** — above the $27.41/MWh time-weighted average. Wind generation is highest when prices are above average, yielding a relatively strong 0.76x value capture.

**3. CAISO SP15 has a higher raw average price but solar never sees it.** The time-weighted average SP15 LMP was $31.00/MWh vs ERCOT HB_WEST's $27.41/MWh, so SP15 *looks* like a better market. But solar production is concentrated entirely in the lowest-priced hours of the day. Solar's value capture ratio of just 0.23x means it earns only 23% of what an always-on baseload asset would earn at the same location.

---

### Monthly Detail

| Month | Wind $/MWh | Solar $/MWh | Winner |
|---|---|---|---|
| Jan | $20.84 | $29.84 | Solar |
| Feb | $12.02 | -$0.23 | Wind |
| Mar | $20.60 | -$21.19 | Wind |
| Apr | $14.46 | -$26.11 | Wind |
| May | $32.24 | -$18.53 | Wind |
| Jun | $20.44 | $8.94 | Wind |
| Jul | $20.38 | $30.87 | Solar |
| Aug | $38.01 | $23.83 | Wind |
| Sep | $23.06 | $17.36 | Wind |
| Oct | $12.58 | $20.11 | Solar |
| Nov | $16.70 | $7.74 | Wind |
| Dec | $24.10 | $25.23 | Solar |

The solar asset had **negative revenue/MWh in four months** (Feb–May), effectively paying to generate during those periods. Wind never had a negative-revenue month. Solar won only 4 of 12 months — those with stronger summer/winter evening net load peaks.

---

### Bottom Line

For a 1 MW asset operating on real-time merchant revenues only in 2024:

- **ERCOT West Wind: $20.90/MWh** captured
- **CAISO SP15 Solar: $7.08/MWh** captured

**The wind asset wins decisively**, capturing roughly 3x the revenue per MWh generated, driven by a much more favorable generation-to-price correlation profile. While both technologies face value deflation in their respective markets, CAISO solar's extreme midday price collapse (with deeply negative prices in spring) makes it the substantially weaker merchant performer.
